days.title: May 7 — Arrival · Royal & River
days.note: Arriving 9:35 · check in · afternoon start · light day

stops.name: The Wolseley
stops.travel: 🚶 2-min walk from Piccadilly Circus or Green Park Tube. On Piccadilly itself, opposite the Ritz.
stops.history: The Wolseley opened in 2003 in one of London's most extraordinary rooms — a vast 1920s palace built for the Wolseley Motor Company as their flagship showroom, designed by William Curtis Green in the style of a Florentine palazzo. The soaring arched ceiling, black-and-white marble floors, and gilded ironwork were designed to impress car buyers in the age of motoring glamour. When the car company failed in 1926, the building became a Barclays Bank branch for decades. Restaurateurs Chris Corbin and Jeremy King transformed it into a grand European café in 2003, modelled on the great Viennese and Parisian cafés — Café Central, Lipp, and the Deux Magots. It rapidly became the social and cultural centre of London, where politicians, authors, artists, and celebrities come for breakfast, and has remained so ever since. It is arguably London's finest all-day café.
stops.todo: 🥐Order for breakfast: The Wolseley Full Breakfast or the Eggs Benedict with smoked salmon. The pastry basket is outstanding. Sit in the main room, not the booths, to absorb the full grandeur of the space. Book at least 2 weeks ahead for weekday breakfast — it fills completely. The atmosphere at 8:30am, with the room buzzing and the coffee service immaculate, is one of London's great experiences.

stops.name: Walk through Mayfair
stops.travel: 🚶 25-min walk south through Mayfair — one of London's finest walking routes.
stops.history: Mayfair takes its name from the May Fair held annually here from 1686 until the 1760s, when it was suppressed for causing disorder. The area was developed into London's most prestigious residential district in the early 18th century by the Grosvenor family, who still own much of it today. Burlington Arcade (1819) was the first purpose-built shopping arcade in Britain. It was commissioned by Lord George Cavendish to stop people throwing oyster shells and rubbish into his garden next door. The Beadles (uniformed guards) who patrol it today date from its opening — they enforce rules against running, singing, and opening umbrellas, which have been in place since 1819. Savile Row, which you'll pass through, has been the global home of bespoke tailoring since the 1800s. The Beatles' last public performance was on the rooftop of No. 3 in January 1969.
stops.todo: 🚶Route: Walk south from Wallace Collection → through Marylebone → along Bond Street → through Burlington Arcade → along Savile Row → arriving at Dishoom Carnaby on Kingly Street. This is one of London's most architecturally rewarding walking routes.

stops.name: The Wallace Collection
stops.address: Hertford House, Manchester Square, London W1U 3BN
stops.travel: 🚶 5-min walk south then east along Wigmore Street to Manchester Square.
stops.history: Hertford House was built in 1776 as Manchester House, one of the grandest private mansions in London. It was acquired by the 2nd Marquess of Hertford in 1797 and the family spent the next century filling it with one of the finest private art collections in history. The 4th Marquess and his illegitimate son Sir Richard Wallace were the great collectors — amassing Rembrandt, Velázquez, Fragonard, Rubens, Titian, and Frans Hals' The Laughing Cavalier (1624). Wallace's widow bequeathed the entire collection to the British nation in 1897 on the condition it never leave London and never be lent out. It has been a free public museum ever since — one of the most generous gifts in art history.
stops.todo: 🎨Must-see rooms: The Great Gallery (top floor) for the Old Masters. Room 22 for Hals' Laughing Cavalier. The European Armoury — 2,500 pieces of medieval armour — is extraordinary. The glass-roofed courtyard café is excellent for a mid-morning stop. Download the free audio guide from their website.

stops.name: Daunt Books + Coffee
stops.travel: 🚶 20-min walk from Piccadilly, or Tube to Baker Street (5 min). Head south on Marylebone High Street — one of London's loveliest village streets.
stops.history: Daunt Books opened in 1990 in this stunning 1910 Edwardian building, originally built as a bookshop for travel writer and publisher James Daunt. The interior is a masterpiece of Edwardian design — a long, oak-galleried hall with a triple-height stained-glass skylight at the rear. The building was designed by architect John Edgar Chapman. Marylebone High Street itself sits on a medieval lane that existed before the Norman Conquest. The name comes from "Mary-le-bourne" — St Mary's Church by the stream (bourne). In the 18th century this was a fashionable village on the edge of London; today it retains that elegant village character.
stops.todo: ☕Order: A flat white or filter coffee from the in-store café. Browse the legendary travel section organised by country — fiction, history, and guidebooks for each destination together on one shelf. Pick up something for the journey home.

stops.name: Sir John Soane's Museum
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk east along High Holborn to Lincoln's Inn Fields — London's largest public square.
stops.history: Sir John Soane (1753–1837) was the greatest British architect of his age — designer of the original Bank of England and Dulwich Picture Gallery. He spent 40 years transforming these three terraced houses into an architectural experiment, knocking through floors and walls to create extraordinary spaces flooded with natural light via mirrors and skylights. The house contains 30,000 objects including an Egyptian sarcophagus of Seti I (1279 BC), Hogarth's complete A Rake's Progress series (1733), Roman antiquities, architectural models, and an entire picture gallery with walls that swing open to reveal paintings three layers deep. Soane left it to the nation on his death in 1837 with the condition it remain exactly as he left it. It has barely changed since.
stops.todo: 🏛️Don't miss: Ask a guide to open the picture planes in the Picture Room to reveal the three hidden layers of paintings. Find Seti I's sarcophagus in the basement Sepulchral Chamber. Look for the monk's cell and the "Padre Giovanni" tomb — Soane's eccentric tribute to his pet dog. Download the audio guide before visiting from soane.org.

stops.name: Two Temple Place
stops.travel: 🚶 10-min walk south along Chancery Lane to the Victoria Embankment. The building is just off the river.
stops.history: Two Temple Place was built in 1895 for William Waldorf Astor, the richest man in America, as his London estate office. Designed by architect John Loughborough Pearson (who also designed Truro Cathedral), it is a stunning example of Late Victorian Gothic Revival architecture. The exterior is covered in a frieze of figures from literature and history. Inside, the main hall features a triple-height mahogany staircase with 52 carved figures representing characters from The Last of the Mohicans. The stained glass, gilded ceilings, and marble floors were all installed at extraordinary expense. Astor subsequently donated it to the nation. It now hosts free contemporary art exhibitions from Britain's regional museum collections — one of London's best-kept secrets.
stops.todo: ✨Explore: Look up at the carved wooden staircase — spot all 52 literary figures. The main hall has extraordinary gilded detail. Check what exhibition is currently running at twotempleplace.org — they are consistently excellent. Closed Mondays.

stops.name: Temple Church
stops.travel: 🚶 5-min walk along the Embankment then into the Temple complex through the archway on Fleet Street.
stops.history: Temple Church was built in 1185 by the Knights Templar — the military monastic order founded in Jerusalem in 1119 to protect Christian pilgrims travelling to the Holy Land. The circular nave was deliberately modelled on the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem. The nine stone effigies of crusader knights on the floor date from 1185–1250. After the Templars were suppressed and their leaders burned at the stake in 1312, the church passed to the Knights Hospitaller and then to the legal profession. It is still owned by the Inner and Middle Temple law societies. The church survived the Great Fire of 1666 but was badly damaged in the Blitz of 1941 — the current interior is a meticulous post-war restoration. The Da Vinci Code used it as a location, but the real history is far stranger than the novel.
stops.todo: ⚔️Look for: The nine crusader knight effigies on the floor — run your eyes over the faces, they are extraordinary portraits. The Round Church (1185) vs the Chancel (1240) — you can see the architectural shift from Romanesque to Early Gothic. Open Mon–Fri only, small entry charge.

stops.name: Postman's Park
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk north along Aldersgate Street — a classic City of London street with layers of history.
stops.history: Postman's Park was created in 1880 on the site of three former churchyards near St Paul's. The name comes from its use as a lunchtime garden by workers from the nearby General Post Office. In 1900, the Victorian artist George Frederic Watts fulfilled a lifelong ambition by installing the Memorial to Heroic Self-Sacrifice — a covered loggia of hand-painted Doulton ceramic tiles, each commemorating an ordinary person who died saving another. The memorial honours people like "Frederick Alfred Croft, Inspector, saved a lunatic woman from suicide at Woolwich Arsenal station, January 11, 1878, but was himself run over by the train". Each tile is a complete world. There are 54 plaques — Watts planned 120 but died before completing the project.
stops.todo: 🌿Take time: Read every single plaque slowly — they are extraordinary miniature stories of ordinary heroism. The stories of children saving siblings are particularly moving. The park is almost completely unknown to tourists despite being 5 minutes from St Paul's. Sit on a bench and absorb it.

stops.name: Leadenhall Market
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk east through the City of London — past the Bank of England and the Royal Exchange.
stops.history: Leadenhall Market has occupied this site since the 14th century — it is the oldest market in the City of London. The site was part of the Roman forum of Londinium, the civic heart of Roman London (founded around 50 AD). Medieval London's main poultry and cheese market stood here. The current magnificent structure was designed by Sir Horace Jones in 1881 — the same architect who designed Tower Bridge. The painted cast-iron roof structure, with its ornate green and burgundy ironwork and soaring glass roof, is one of the finest Victorian engineering achievements in London. It was used as Diagon Alley in the first Harry Potter film (2001) — the optician's shop that doubled as the Leaky Cauldron still stands on the corner.
stops.todo: 🍷Stop for: A glass of wine or cocktail at one of the bars under the arches. Look up at the painted ceiling — the detail is extraordinary. Find the corner that appeared in Harry Potter. The market is at its best in the late afternoon when City workers fill the bars.

stops.name: Sky Garden — Sunset Cocktails
stops.travel: 🚶 2-min walk — the Sky Garden is in the Walkie-Talkie building (20 Fenchurch Street), directly next to Leadenhall Market.
stops.history: The Walkie-Talkie building (officially 20 Fenchurch Street) was designed by Rafael Viñoly and completed in 2014. It is nicknamed the Walkie-Talkie because of its distinctive widening shape. In 2013, before the glass was treated, it famously focused sunlight so intensely on the street below that it melted a car and fried eggs on the pavement — earning it the tabloid nickname "the Fryscraper". The Sky Garden on floors 35–37 was added as a public benefit — a requirement for planning permission — and opened in 2015. It is now one of London's most popular free attractions, offering some of the finest panoramic views of the city, including St Paul's Cathedral, the Shard, Tower Bridge, and the entire sweep of the Thames.
stops.todo: 🌅Arrive at sunset for the most spectacular views as the city lights come on. The two outdoor terraces give you unobstructed 360° views. Order cocktails at the bar and watch the sun set over St Paul's. MUST BOOK AHEAD at skygarden.london — slots release 3 weeks in advance and sell out within hours. Free entry.
stops.prebookText: Book free entry — sells out 3 weeks ahead

days.title: May 8 — Greenwich & the East
days.note: Full day out — Thames Clipper to Greenwich, back via Tower Bridge & Crown Jewels

stops.name: Ottolenghi Notting Hill — Breakfast
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube from Piccadilly Circus to Notting Hill Gate (District/Circle, 8 min). Walk 10 min north to Ledbury Road — one of London's loveliest neighbourhood streets.
stops.history: Ottolenghi was founded in 2002 by Yotam Ottolenghi and Sami Tamimi — one Israeli, one Palestinian, both raised in Jerusalem on opposite sides of the city. Their food is a vivid expression of shared Middle Eastern and Mediterranean heritage. Ledbury Road is one of London's most beautiful neighbourhood streets — Georgian townhouses in pastel colours, independent boutiques, and the relaxed atmosphere of Notting Hill before tourists arrive.
stops.todo: 🥗Order: Poached eggs with labneh and za'atar, seasonal grain bowls, or freshly baked pastries with tahini. Everything is clean, bold, beautifully presented. Note: closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays — check before going.
stops.prebookText: Book ahead or arrive at opening — closed Tue/Wed

stops.name: Leighton House Museum
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to High Street Kensington (District/Circle line, 20 min from London Bridge). Walk 10 min north through Holland Park.
stops.history: Leighton House was built between 1864 and 1895 for Frederic Leighton (1830–1896), the most celebrated British painter of the Victorian era and President of the Royal Academy. Leighton was obsessed with the Islamic world and spent decades collecting tiles, ceramics, and architectural fragments from across the Middle East. The centrepiece is the Arab Hall (1877–1881), a room of extraordinary beauty designed to display Leighton's collection of Islamic tiles — 1,000 tiles dating from the 9th to the 17th century, gathered from Damascus, Cairo, Rhodes, and Syria. The golden mosaic frieze was designed by Walter Crane. A marble fountain tinkles in the centre beneath a gilded dome. Nothing in Britain prepares you for walking into this room.
stops.todo: 🕌Don't miss: Stand in the Arab Hall doorway for the full impact before entering. Look up at the gilded mosaic ceiling. Find the 12th-century Syrian tiles mixed with Victorian reproductions — can you tell the difference? The garden is beautiful in spring. The café in the former studio serves excellent coffee. ~£10 entry. Closed Tuesdays.
stops.prebookText: No pre-booking needed, but check for closures

stops.name: Barbican Centre — Art & Architecture
stops.travel: 🚶 10-min walk south from Bunhill Fields — or take the Tube one stop to Barbican.
stops.history: The Barbican Estate is the largest single arts and residential complex in Europe, built between 1965 and 1982 by architects Chamberlin, Powell and Bon for the City of London Corporation. It was a utopian vision of urban living — 6,500 residents in a self-contained city within the city, complete with restaurants, theatres, cinemas, gardens, and a lake. The site was chosen because this entire area — some 35 acres — was completely flattened by the Blitz in December 1940. Before that it was a dense medieval and Victorian neighbourhood. Before that, it was the location of the Cripplegate Fort, a Roman fortification that pre-dated the main City walls. You can still see sections of the original Roman wall incorporated into the Barbican's structure. The Barbican Centre opened in 1982 and is home to the London Symphony Orchestra.
stops.todo: 🏗️Explore: Walk the elevated walkways (called "highwalks") — designed so residents could walk from home to the arts centre without touching the ground. Visit the Barbican Conservatory (check opening times — usually Sundays and occasional weekdays) — one of London's largest tropical indoor gardens. Check the free gallery exhibition. Have a coffee at the Barbican Kitchen overlooking the lake.

stops.name: Bunhill Fields Burial Ground
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk west through Shoreditch and Old Street.
stops.history: Bunhill Fields has been a burial ground since at least the 13th century. The name derives from "Bone Hill" — a reference to the enormous quantity of human bones moved here from the charnel house of St Paul's Cathedral in 1549. During the Great Plague of 1665, over 120,000 bodies were buried in mass graves here. From 1665 it became the principal burial ground for Nonconformists — Protestant dissenters who refused to conform to the Church of England. Three of Britain's greatest writers are buried here within metres of each other: William Blake (1757–1827, poet and artist), Daniel Defoe (1660–1731, author of Robinson Crusoe), and John Bunyan (1628–1688, author of The Pilgrim's Progress). John Wesley, founder of Methodism, is buried across the road.
stops.todo: 📚Find the graves of: William Blake (distinctive headstone near the north wall), Daniel Defoe (tall obelisk), and John Bunyan (elaborate tomb). Stand between them — three of the most influential writers in English literature, all buried in a park most Londoners have never visited. Allow 25 minutes.

stops.name: Rochelle Canteen — Brunch
stops.travel: 🚶 Arrive via Arnold Circus — the centrepiece bandstand surrounded by the Boundary Estate, the world's first council housing (1900).
stops.history: Rochelle Canteen opened in 2004 in a converted Victorian school bike shed — part of the Rochelle School building (1883), a former London County Council school. To find it you must ring a bell on a plain brick wall; there is no sign. It was opened by chefs Melanie Arnold and Margot Henderson (wife of Fergus Henderson of St John's fame). Arnold Circus outside is the centrepiece of the Boundary Estate — built in 1900 and the world's first social housing project. The bandstand stands on a mound made from the rubble of the Old Nichol slum it replaced. The estate is a masterpiece of Arts and Crafts social architecture.
stops.todo: 🍳Order (healthy): Whatever fish or vegetables are on that day — the menu changes constantly based on what's freshest. The sea bass, seasonal grain dishes, and mushroom plates are consistently outstanding. Fresh and clean flavours — no heavy sauces. Ring the bell on the gate to enter. Book well ahead.
stops.prebookText: Book online — small restaurant, fills fast

stops.name: Walk through Spitalfields & Brick Lane
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk west through Bethnal Green into Spitalfields — one of London's most historically layered neighbourhoods.
stops.history: Spitalfields has been London's immigrant quarter for 350 years. The name comes from the Hospital Fields of the medieval priory of St Mary Spital (1197). Brick Lane tells the story of London's immigrants in a single street. The building at the corner of Fournier Street has been, in sequence: a Huguenot chapel (1743), a Wesleyan chapel (1819), the Great Synagogue for Jewish immigrants (1898), and now the Jamme Masjid mosque (1976) — each community transforming it for their own faith. No other building in London tells the story of immigration so vividly. The Georgian terraced houses on Fournier Street and Elder Street were built by Huguenot silk merchants in the 1720s–30s and are among the finest unaltered 18th-century streetscapes in London.
stops.todo: 🚶Walk via: Bethnal Green Road → Brick Lane (look for the multi-faith building on the corner of Fournier Street) → Elder Street (extraordinary Georgian terrace) → arriving at Rochelle Canteen on Arnold Circus. This 15-minute walk is one of the most historically rich in London.

stops.name: Maltby Street Market — Lunch
stops.address: Arch 46, Ropewalk, Maltby St, London SE1 3PA
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to London Bridge (5 min from Barbican). Walk 12 min south under the railway arches along Bermondsey Street.
stops.history: Bermondsey has been a market and trade district since the medieval period. The area was famous for its leather tanning industry from the 12th century — the smell was so bad that it was kept south of the river. The tanneries that supplied leather to Victorian London's book-binding and shoe industries operated in these streets until the 20th century. Maltby Street Market began informally around 2010 as a handful of artisan food producers setting up under the Victorian railway arches — the same arches built for the London & Greenwich Railway (1836), the world's first purpose-built commuter railway. Unlike Borough Market (which has become a tourist destination), Maltby Street remains genuinely local — most traders are independent producers who make what they sell.
stops.todo: 🥗Order: Ethiopian injera with slow-cooked stews, fresh fish tacos, handmade empanadas, or seasonal vegetable dishes — all healthy and outstanding. Get a glass of natural wine at one of the tiny arch bars and eat at the outdoor tables. Far better and less crowded than Borough Market. Open Sat 10–5, Sun 11–4.

stops.name: Dennis Severs' House — Candlelit Evening
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to Liverpool Street (Central line, 20 min). Walk 10 min west through Spitalfields.
stops.history: Dennis Severs (1948–1999) was an American artist and performer who bought this Georgian townhouse in Spitalfields in 1979 for £50 and spent 20 years transforming it into what he called a "still-life drama." He invented the Jervis family — a fictional family of Huguenot silk weavers — and staged their lives across the 10 rooms of the house across three centuries. The house is always in a state of being "just left" — fires smoldering, food half-eaten, chamber pots recently used, candles guttering. You move through in silence, from the 17th-century basement kitchen to the Victorian top-floor garret, as if the family has just stepped out. The experience is part historical recreation, part theatrical installation, part meditation on time and impermanence. Severs described it as "you either see it or you don't." Most visitors are deeply moved. Nothing else in London compares.
stops.todo: 🕯️Rules: You must be silent throughout. No photography. Move slowly through each room — Severs said the house rewards people who look at everything. The smell of woodsmoke, candle wax, and old food is part of the experience. The evening sessions (5pm–8:15pm) are candlelit and the most atmospheric. Available Fri and Sat evenings only. Book online well in advance — very limited slots.
stops.prebookText: Book online — very limited Fri/Sat evening slots

stops.name: Duck & Waffle — Rooftop Dinner
stops.travel: 🚶 5-min walk north from Sky Garden to Heron Tower on Bishopsgate.
stops.history: Heron Tower (now officially Salesforce Tower) is one of the City of London's tallest buildings at 230 metres, completed in 2011. It was designed by architects Kohn Pedersen Fox and features a distinctive external structural frame. Duck & Waffle opened in 2012 and was one of London's first 24-hour fine dining restaurants. At 40 floors up, it holds one of the most dramatic dining positions in the city — views of Tower Bridge, the Thames, the Gherkin, and on clear nights, all the way to the North Downs.
stops.todo: 🍽️Order (healthy options): Seasonal vegetable dishes, fresh fish, and the tasting menus. Request a window table when booking — insist on it. The city views at night are extraordinary. Open 24 hours; reserve the 8pm slot for the best light transition from dusk to night.

days.title: May 9 — South Bank, City & Soho
days.note: Friday — Borough Market lunch, Globe, Sky Garden, British Museum, Chinatown

stops.name: Colbert — Breakfast
stops.type: Breakfast · French brasserie · 10 min from Piccadilly
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube from Piccadilly Circus to Sloane Square (District/Circle, 10 min). Colbert is right at the station exit on the corner of the square.
stops.history: Colbert opened in 2011, modelled on the great Parisian brasseries — democratic institutions where Parisians breakfasted since the Belle Époque. It is run by the same team as The Wolseley. Sloane Square is one of London's most elegant formal squares, completed in the 1770s. The King's Road running west from here was Henry VIII's private road to Hampton Court, later becoming the epicentre of 1970s punk fashion.
stops.todo: ☕Order: Oeufs en cocotte (baked eggs with herbs), avocado and smoked salmon on toast, granola with seasonal fruit. Café au lait is excellent. Proper French brasserie breakfast — elegant, unhurried. Book ahead on weekends.

stops.name: Lunch — Farmacy or NOPI
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk north through Notting Hill from Holland Park Gate.
stops.history: Westbourne Grove and the surrounding Notting Hill area underwent a dramatic transformation from the 1990s onwards — from a somewhat run-down neighbourhood to one of London's most fashionable addresses. The area's diversity is rooted in the Windrush generation of Caribbean immigrants who settled here from the late 1940s, and is celebrated annually in the Notting Hill Carnival (established 1966), Europe's largest street festival. Farmacy is a plant-based restaurant that pioneered London's healthy dining movement when it opened in 2016. NOPI (Ottolenghi's restaurant on Warwick Street, slightly east) is one of London's finest Middle Eastern-influenced restaurants.
stops.todo: 🥑Farmacy (74 Westbourne Grove): Outstanding plant-based food — jackfruit tacos, miso-glazed aubergine, excellent grain bowls. Beautiful interior. Book ahead. OR NOPI (21-22 Warwick St, W1B): Ottolenghi's flagship restaurant — more refined than the delis, exceptional vegetable-forward dishes. Both are healthy and genuinely delicious.
stops.prebookText: Book Farmacy or NOPI in advance

stops.name: Walk through Kensington & Hyde Park
stops.travel: 🚶 Walk east from Notting Hill through Kensington to Hyde Park — about 20 minutes.
stops.history: Hyde Park was seized from the monks of Westminster Abbey by Henry VIII in 1536 as a private hunting ground. It was opened to the public by Charles I in 1637 and has been London's great democratic outdoor space ever since — the site of mass protests, the Great Exhibition of 1851, anti-war rallies, and free concerts. Speakers' Corner at the northeast corner has been a legal site for public oratory since 1872 — Marx, Lenin, George Orwell, and William Morris have all spoken here. The Serpentine (the lake) was created in 1730 by damming the River Westbourne — which now flows underground through Chelsea. The Albert Memorial (1872) sits on the south edge of the park — a gilded monument to Queen Victoria's beloved husband Prince Albert, who died aged 42 in 1861. The Royal Albert Hall opposite was built as part of his legacy.
stops.todo: 🌳Walk via: Kensington Gardens → the Albert Memorial → along the Serpentine → up to Speakers' Corner → across to Mayfair. This is one of London's great walks — the park is at its most beautiful in May when everything is in bloom. About 40 minutes of walking.

stops.name: The Wallace Collection
stops.address: Hertford House, Manchester Square, London W1U 3BN
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to Bond Street (Central or Jubilee line). Walk 8 min north through Marylebone.
stops.history: If not visited on Day 1 — see the Day 1 entry for the full history. One of the greatest art museums on earth, housed in an 18th-century private mansion. The Laughing Cavalier, Rembrandt's Titus, and three floors of European masterpieces, all free, all in a building that feels like a private ducal palace. The glass-roofed courtyard café is excellent for a mid-morning pause.
stops.todo: 🎨If you've already been on Day 1: Replace this with a visit to Daunt Books (5-min walk) for a longer browse, or walk through Marylebone Village — the high street has excellent healthy food shops, pharmacies, and cafés. Then proceed directly to Leighton House.

stops.name: Sketch — Afternoon Tea
stops.travel: 🚶 10-min walk from the east side of Hyde Park through Mayfair to Conduit Street.
stops.history: Sketch occupies a listed Georgian townhouse on Conduit Street, originally built as a suite of townhouses in the 1770s. The building has housed private members' clubs, tailors, and restaurants over its 250-year history. The current incarnation opened in 2002 under the creative direction of French chef Pierre Gagnaire and restaurateur Mourad Mazouz. In 2014, the Gallery room was redesigned by artist David Shrigley — the walls are now covered in his distinctive hand-drawn illustrations. The egg-pod bathrooms were designed as sculptural objects by artist Mike Figgis and became one of London's most photographed interiors. Conduit Street was named for the water conduit installed here by the Corporation of London in 1500 to carry fresh water from the fields of Marylebone into the City.
stops.todo: 🫖Book The Parlour room for afternoon tea — refillable finger sandwiches (ask for vegetarian), extraordinary pastries, and 14 teas to choose from. A live violinist plays. The egg-pod bathrooms must be visited. Don't rush — the experience is designed to last 2 hours. A perfect romantic treat 10 minutes walk from your Piccadilly hotel. Book via sketch.london.
stops.prebookText: Book The Parlour room — book 2 weeks ahead

stops.name: Evening walk back through St James's
stops.travel: 🚶 15-min walk south from sketch through Mayfair and into St James's Park.
stops.history: St James's Park is the oldest of the Royal Parks, created by Henry VIII in 1536 on marshland drained from the Tyburn stream. Charles II redesigned it in 1660 in the French formal style after returning from exile in Paris. It is now a wildlife sanctuary in the heart of government London — pelicans have been kept here since 1664, a gift from the Russian ambassador. The view from the bridge across the lake — looking east toward Whitehall, west toward Buckingham Palace — is one of the great London views, particularly in the evening light. The Mall alongside the park was laid out in 1660 as a croquet ground (the game was called "pall-mall"). The houses of St James's to the north include Christie's auction house and several of London's oldest gentlemen's clubs.
stops.todo: 🌆Evening walk: From sketch → south through Mayfair → along the south edge of Green Park → through the gates into St James's Park → across the bridge (pause for the view) → back north to Piccadilly. About 30 minutes of the most beautiful evening walking in London. The park lights at dusk are magical.

stops.name: Walk through Islington & Clerkenwell
stops.travel: 🚶 20-min walk south through Islington and Clerkenwell to the Wallace Collection Tube stop.
stops.history: Clerkenwell was one of medieval London's most important districts — home to the Knights Hospitaller (rivals to the Knights Templar) whose priory, St John's, gave London its ambulance service (St John Ambulance). The name Clerkenwell means "Clerks' Well" — a spring used by medieval students and clergy. In the 18th and 19th centuries, Clerkenwell became London's watchmaking and clockmaking quarter — the precision engineering skills brought by Huguenot refugees who settled here after fleeing France in 1685. Many of the small workshops survive in the back streets. Exmouth Market, which you'll pass through, is a pedestrianised street of independent food stalls and restaurants that captures the neighbourhood's current character perfectly.
stops.todo: 🚶Walk via: Upper Street → Exmouth Market (coffee stop if needed) → Farringdon Road → Hatton Garden (London's diamond and jewellery quarter since the 16th century) → Holborn → Tube to Bond Street for the Wallace Collection. About 20 minutes of fascinating back streets.

stops.name: Barbican Centre — Evening Concert or Theatre
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube from Green Park to Barbican (Jubilee to Bank, then Northern, 25 min). Arrive by 7:15 for pre-show drinks.
stops.history: The Barbican Centre is the most ambitious arts centre in Europe — 10 venues including the 2,000-seat Barbican Hall (home of the London Symphony Orchestra), the Barbican Theatre (home of the RSC's London productions), a cinema, art gallery, conservatory, and three restaurants. It was built specifically to bring culture to what had been a bombed-out wasteland. The architecture — vast concrete terraces, elevated walkways, and a man-made lake — was intended to create a self-contained urban world where art was woven into daily life. When it opened in 1982, the Queen said she hoped it would become "one of the wonders of the modern world."
stops.todo: 🎶Book in advance: Check barbican.org.uk for the programme on your date — the LSO performs regularly here, and the quality is extraordinary. The Barbican also hosts world-class jazz, international theatre, and film retrospectives. Pre-show drinks at the Barbican Kitchen overlooking the lake are excellent. A perfect final evening in London.

days.title: May 10 — Parks, Mayfair & Farewell
days.note: Last full day — gentle parks morning, afternoon tea, farewell dinner

stops.name: Borough Market — Breakfast
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to London Bridge (Jubilee/Northern line). 2-min walk. Or walk across London Bridge from the City.
stops.history: Borough Market has existed on this site since at least 1014 — it is one of the oldest food markets in the world. The current Victorian iron and glass structure was built in the 1850s, funded by the market's own traders. During the Great Fire of London in 1666, the market survived because Southwark was south of the river. The market declined significantly through most of the 20th century but was revived from the 1990s onward, becoming one of London's premier food destinations. Today it hosts over 100 traders selling bread, cheese, charcuterie, fresh produce, street food, and specialist ingredients from across Britain and Europe. It is a working wholesale market as well as a retail destination.
stops.todo: 🧀For breakfast: Get a freshly baked pastry from Bread Ahead bakery, a salt beef bagel from Monmouth Coffee + the food stalls, or a full hot breakfast from one of the market kitchens. Arrive before 10am — it gets extremely busy by 11am. The cheese and charcuterie counters are excellent for last-day picnic supplies.

stops.name: Tate Modern
stops.type: World's greatest modern art museum · Free
stops.travel: 🚶 10-min walk west along the South Bank from Borough Market, following the river.
stops.history: Tate Modern opened in 2000 in the former Bankside Power Station, designed by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott (who also designed Battersea Power Station and the classic red telephone box). The building was decommissioned in 1981 after generating electricity for London for 30 years. Swiss architects Herzog & de Meuron converted it into a gallery, preserving the vast Turbine Hall as one of the world's most dramatic public spaces. The Switch House extension (2016) added ten more floors, giving the gallery views across the Thames to St Paul's. The permanent collection includes Picasso, Rothko, Bacon, Hirst, Bourgeois, and Warhol — all free.
stops.todo: 🎨Don't miss: The Turbine Hall (always a spectacular commission), the Rothko Room on Level 3, and the viewing platform on Level 10 for the best panoramic view of London — St Paul's, the City, and the Thames all at once. The café on the riverside terrace is excellent for a final London coffee.

stops.name: Walk the South Bank & Millennium Bridge
stops.travel: 🚶 Walk from Tate Modern — the Millennium Bridge is right outside the gallery.
stops.history: The Millennium Bridge was designed by Norman Foster and Anthony Caro, opened in June 2000, and immediately nicknamed the Wobbly Bridge after it swayed alarmingly when 80,000 pedestrians walked across it on opening day. It was closed for two years while engineers added dampers. It has not wobbled since. The view from the bridge — St Paul's Cathedral to the north, the City skyline, and the South Bank to the south — is one of London's most photographed perspectives. The walk along the South Bank from here past the Globe Theatre, the Tate, and toward London Bridge is one of the finest riverside walks in Europe.
stops.todo: 📸Walk the bridge for the classic St Paul's view — the best photograph of the trip. Then walk east along the South Bank past the Globe Theatre (you can do a self-guided exterior visit free), past Clink Street (London's oldest prison site), back toward Borough Market for any last purchases before heading to the hotel.

stops.name: Sketch — Afternoon Tea
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to Oxford Circus (Central/Victoria/Bakerloo line). 5-min walk south through Mayfair to Conduit Street.
stops.history: Sketch was opened in 2002 by restaurateur Mourad Mazouz in a former Scottish Presbyterian church built in 1779, later converted into a Regency townhouse. The building was completely reimagined by artist and designer Martin Creed and others. The Parlour room, where afternoon tea is served, is decorated in blush pink and features live classical music from a resident violinist on most afternoons. The egg-shaped pod bathrooms in the basement are among the most famous in London. Sketch has won multiple Michelin stars across its different restaurant spaces.
stops.todo: 🫖Book The Parlour specifically (not the Gallery or Lecture Room). Request a table near the violinist. The afternoon tea is one of London's finest — exceptional pastries, finger sandwiches, and a theatrical setting that makes it a perfect farewell experience. Book 1–2 weeks ahead. Arrive 10 minutes early.
stops.prebookText: Book The Parlour — request window or violinist table

stops.name: Burlington Arcade & Bond Street — Final Shopping
stops.travel: 🚶 5-min walk from Sketch, south on New Bond Street to Piccadilly.
stops.history: Burlington Arcade (1819) was the first purpose-built shopping arcade in Britain, built by Lord Cavendish to prevent passers-by from throwing oyster shells into his garden next door. The Beadle guards in their top hats and frock coats still patrol today, enforcing the original rules against running, whistling, and opening umbrellas — in place since 1819. The arcade houses British luxury brands including cashmere specialists, jewellers, and perfumers.
stops.todo: 🛍️Last chance for British cashmere, fragrances, and luxury gifts. N.Peal cashmere is excellent. Browse the arcade and then walk north on Bond Street past the couture houses. End at Fortnum & Mason on Piccadilly (5-min walk) for tea, biscuits, and quintessentially British gifts to take home.

stops.name: Rules Restaurant — Farewell Dinner
stops.travel: 🚇 Tube to Covent Garden (Piccadilly line). 5-min walk south on Maiden Lane.
stops.history: Rules is London's oldest restaurant, established in 1798 by Thomas Rule on Maiden Lane, Covent Garden. It has been in continuous operation for over 225 years and has served virtually every notable figure in British history: Charles Dickens, Graham Greene, Evelyn Waugh, King Edward VII and his mistress Lillie Langtry (who had a private room), John Betjeman, and countless others. The interior has barely changed in a century — dark oak panelling, red banquettes, walls covered in cartoons, prints, and paintings from Victorian and Edwardian London. The menu specialises in traditional British game and seasonal produce from the restaurant's own estate in the Pennines.
stops.todo: 🍷Order: The game pie or beef Wellington are the signature dishes. Order the potted shrimps to start — they're a British classic. The wine list has excellent burgundies. Request a booth or a corner table. This is the most historically atmospheric restaurant in London and a perfect final dinner — you are eating in the same room as Dickens.
stops.prebookText: Book online — request a booth or the Dickens room
